data.title: 5 takeaways from Trump's Cabinet meeting as Iran war wages
data.description:
President Trump held his 11th Cabinet meeting of his second term  on Thursday, with the war in Iran dominating most of the discussion. 

Unlike past Cabinet meetings, Thursday’s lasted just over an hour and half with only a few secretaries taking time to speak instead of every official in the room.

The meeting was also the first for Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin, who was sworn-in to his post on Tuesday. 

Here are five takeaways from the Cabinet meeting: 

Witkoff confirms 15-point peace plan

Trump’s chief special envoy Steve Witkoff confirmed the U.S. has presented a 15 point “action list” that the U.S. intends to serve as framework for a peace plan with Iran during the meeting. 

“I can report to you today that we have, along with your foreign policy team, presented a 15 point action list that forms the framework of a peace deal,” Witkoff said. 

“This has been circulated through the Pakistani government acting as the mediator,” he continued, saying it resulted in “strong and productive talks.” 

Witkoff described the talks as “sensitive, diplomatic solutions,” noting that Trump has directed his officials and the administration to “not negotiate with the news media.” 

“I can say this, we will see where things lead and if we can convince Iran that this is the inflection point,” he said. 

When asked about the prior day’s reporting on such a plan, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters on Wednesday that nothing should be considered confirmed unless it comes from the White House.  

“I’ve seen a lot of speculation and reporting about potential talks that could happen later this week,” Leavitt said. “Nothing should be deemed official until it is announced formally by the White House.”

Iran, for its part, responded this week with its own demands including the war coming to an end, payments for war damages and reparations and Iranian sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz.

So far, proposals from both sides are unlikely to be accepted by either the U.S. or Iran.

Hegseth says US would continue ‘negotiating with bombs

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said he welcomes a potential peace agreement with Iran, but warned the U.S. would continue to use military force against the country, marking another pinpoint in the dual messaging coming from the White House on what its intentions may be.

“We pray for a deal and we welcome a deal, and we have the ultimate dealmaker to make it happen,” Hegseth said, referring to Trump sitting beside him.

“But in the meantime, as I said yesterday, the Department of War will continue negotiating with bombs,” he continued. 

Hegseth’s comments are reflective of recent messaging from the administration on the war. Earlier on Thursday, Trump warned in a TruthSocial post that “it won’t be pretty” if Iran does not “get serious soon” in negotiating with the U.S. 

On Wednesday, Leavitt told reporters that Washington’s “core objectives” had been met in Iran, but warned the U.S. would increase attacks if Iran did not agree to U.S. terms. 

Trump lays blame on Democrats for DHS shutdown

Trump laid blame on Democrats for the ongoing shutdown at the Department of Homeland Security, warning he would take “drastic measures” if the shutdown is not ended in the near future. 

“We’re now on day 41 of the disgraceful Democrat shutdown of Markwayne’s department, Congratulations,” Trump said. 

“Democrats are really punishing the American people,” he continued, saying that the American people are blaming Senate Democrats. 

“I saw today on one of the shows where they were interviewing at the airport and they’re all angry at the Democrats,” he said. “They’re actually angry at Schumer, I didn’t think anybody knew his name.” 

“They need to end the shutdown immediately or we’ll have to take some very drastic measures,” he said. 

The impasse on funding the department has led to long, chaotic lines at airports due to Transportation Security Administration (TSA) employees not being paid and calling out of work. 

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-N.D.) said on Thursday that Senate Republicans had sent their “last and final” offer to Democrats to end the shutdown. 

Trump details oil ‘present’ from Iran

Trump revealed at the Cabinet meeting that the “present” Iran gave to the U.S. to prove that they were serious about discussing an end to the war was allowing eight oil tankers to sail through the Strait of Hormuz.

“They said to show you the fact that we’re real, and solid and we’re there, we’re going to let you have eight boats of oil, eight boats, eight big boats of oil,” he said.

He said he was watching Fox News when he saw the eight boats sailing through the strait.

“And I said, ‘Well, I guess they were right,’ and they were and they were real,” he said. “I think they were Pakistani flagged. And I said, ‘Well, I guess we’re dealing with the right people.’”

Trump said Iran then let two more boats sail through the strait, bringing the total to 10 as a way to “apologize for something they said.”

The president then said to Witkoff that he hopes he hadn’t “screwed up” any negotiations with Iran by sharing the gift.

The closure of the strait, which typically sees the passage of roughly 20 million barrels of oil a day, has caused the price of oil and gasoline to skyrocket.

Trump hinted on Tuesday that Iran had given the U.S. a present that had to do with oil and gas and that it was “worth a tremendous amount of money,” but declined to say more.

Trump addresses his White House ballroom

Trump talked about the projects that he’s done around Washington, most notably his 90,000-square-foot ballroom that he has said will seat nearly 1,000 people.

The construction has been ongoing since  the demolition of the East Wing last year, which caused controversy as photos of the demolition became public.

“This ballroom is going to be something. It’s so beautiful for this city, so desperately needed by presidents,” he said. “Now it’s no secret the military wanted it more than anybody. It was supposed to be secret but it became un-secret because of people that are really unpatriotic.”

It is expected to be completed in the summer of 2028, and cost around $400 million, all paid for by private donors, according to Trump.

“It’s all donations. Rich people and people are putting up the money,” he said. “Zero taxpayer dollars.”

He went on to mention a planned triumphal arch in Washington’s Memorial Circle, saying it will be “incredible for the city.”

The project, dubbed “Independence Arch” is said to be completed before July 4, 2026, the country’s 250th birthday, when the president has a whole slew of events planned to celebrate.

Trump even brought up the Kennedy Center, which is planned to be closed for two years as renovations are done starting last month. 

The performing arts center was renamed by its board late last year adding Trump’s name. The building now says “The Donald J. Trump and The John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts.”

He said Thursday that he was “honored” that the board changed the name.

data.title: Homan to Democrats wanting ICE to give up masks: 'You know what? No, we're not!'
data.description:
Border czar Tom Homan on Wednesday said Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents will continue wearing masks despite Democrats’ push to see face coverings removed during immigration operations. 

“You want us to give up masks, well, you know what? No we’re not…,” Homan said during a Wednesday conversation on the “Cats & Cosby Show” on WABC 770 AM. 

“I’m not putting one ICE officer at risk,” he added.

Homan and former Department of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem have shared concerns about agents being doxxed and exposed to retaliation for their role in immigration operations. 

President Trump also said he was a proponent of masks in a Monday Truth Social post, defending face coverings for the ICE agents who are “forced to deal with hardened criminals.”

However, Democrats have argued agents’s concealed identity prevents them from being held accountable by the public. 

Democrats have refused to fund DHS without reforms to ICE operations including the removal of face masks, use of judicial warrants and body worn cameras. That’s led to a long shutdown of DHS, which also houses the Coast Guard and Transportation Security Administration, among other agencies. TSA lines have made trips to airports around the country miserable in recent days.

Republicans have held firm to their decision not to partially fund the agency while lawmakers debate permanent changes to ICE and Customs Border Patrol.

Trump has sent ICE agents to help fill in the gap for TSA, and asked them to forgo their masks while assisting at airports. 

On Wednesday, Homan said if the threats go down, the Trump administration would “think about” removing masks from ICE agents. 

“Right now, I’m not going to do a thing to put our [agents] at risk …,” Homan told hosts John Catsimatidis and Rita Cosby.

“If [Congress] really believe[s] in what they’re doing, pass a law that [during] a government shut down, members of Congress don’t get paid, and let’s see how many times they shut down the government,” he added.

data.title: Man drives through gate at Florida airport, tries to board multiple planes
data.description:
DAYTONA BEACH, Fla. (WFLA) — A man drove through a gate at Daytona Beach International Airport on Wednesday and tried to board multiple planes before airport security caught up to him, Florida officials say.

The man, identified as 58-year-old Bryan Parker, was highly intoxicated when the incident unfolded around 4:25 p.m. at the airport’s international terminal, according to the Volusia County Sheriff’s Office.

Witnesses said Parker’s blue Ford Mustang entered the runway and nearly struck a taxiing plane being used by the Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University.

Deputies said Parker tried to get on an occupied, running airplane. The plane, however, was locked, and he was unable to board.

Parker ran to another airplane, but an airport operations technician chased him, pulled him out of the plane and sat him on a truck’s tailgate, VSO said. He jumped off of the tailgate and ran toward another plane before security stopped him and placed him in handcuffs, according to the sheriff’s office.

Deputies found Parker’s car destroyed when they arrived at the gate, VSO said.

“I went to an AA meeting, and next thing I know, I’m doing cocaine, drinking and smoking pot,” Parker said when authorities questioned him.

“I’m lucky I didn’t get on my … motorcycle,” the suspect added.

Parker was booked into the Volusia County Branch Jail and was charged with attempted aircraft piracy, burglary of a conveyance, felony trespass, exposure of sexual organ, criminal mischief, DUI property damage and refusal to submit to testing.

Operations at the airport continued without disruption, according to officials.

data.title: Newsom: Idea that he and Harris don't like each other 'preposterous'
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) on Thursday dismissed the idea that he and longtime ally former Vice President Kamala Harris do not like each other, calling it “preposterous.”

Newsom praised Harris in an interview with Axios’s Alex Thompson, who asked the governor, “Why don’t you guys like each other?”

“A preposterous premise of a question,” he replied. “Because we absolutely do.”

The governor recalled Harris’s account that after former President Biden endorsed her after ending his 2024 reelection bid, she called Newsom and asked for his support.

He has since confirmed that he texted her back, saying he was hiking and would return her call later. Harris wrote that he did not call back. Still, Newsom issued an endorsement three hours later.

“She gave me a little shot as it relates to the actual truth of the walk I was on,” the California leader told Axios. “And I remember texting her back, I said, ‘Kamala –– I’ve already put out a statement supporting you. I’m the last person you need to talk to.'”

“But for whatever reason, she added that in, and I think it created some color for the book,” he continued. “It certainly helped her book sales.”

Newsom added with a laugh that he had not “fully absorbed her memoir,” and that he was “more focused” on his own, called “Young Man in a Hurry.”

Newsom made similar comments to NBC News’s Kristen Welker on “Meet the Press,” telling her that he did not know why any reference of the hike “was even in the book.”

The governor told Thompson that his friendship with Harris goes back more than 20 years, “before we were both in politics, when we spent time together, on vacations with mutual friends out of the country.”

He noted that when he was elected mayor of San Francisco and she was elected as the city’s district attorney in 2003, they attended each other’s swearing-in ceremonies. Newsom added that he has a “relationship to that relationship.”

“That when she goes, she goes first,” he continued. “And I’ve always understood that and that’s wonderful. And I think the press and pundits want to make something of that, and so they try to color that in.”

Both California Democrats are seen as possible contenders in the 2028 Democratic presidential primary. Just more than a quarter of Golden State Democrats, at 28 percent, say they would back Newsom for president, according to a Berkeley Institute of Governmental Studies poll released Friday.

Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) and former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg followed behind with 14 percent and 11 percent, respectively. According to the survey, Harris comes in fourth place with 9 percent of voters saying they would back her.

data.title: Joe Wilson 'fine' after hitting head in fall at DC home, office says
data.description:
Rep. Joe Wilson (R-S.C.) is recovering after suffering a fall at his Washington, D.C., home on Wednesday evening, according to his office.

A spokesperson for Wilson told The Hill that the 78-year-old lawmaker slipped in the bathroom and cut his head.

“He received stitches, is fine and working remotely,” the spokesperson said.

In September 2024, Wilson was hospitalized after collapsing during a Ukraine Independence Day celebration in Washington, an episode he later said was caused by a severe drop in blood pressure.

South Carolina Attorney General Alan Wilson (R), Wilson’s son and a candidate for the GOP nomination for governor, said at the time that doctors confirmed his father had “stroke-like symptoms.” The elder Wilson received a pacemaker before returning to work.

Rep. Wilson formally filed for reelection to South Carolina’s 2nd Congressional District on March 16. He was first elected to the seat in 2001, making him one of the longest-serving members of the House.  

DEVELOPING.

data.title: AI could finally make congressional hearings work better
data.description:
Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) recently did something no member of Congress has done before. He sat down with Claude, the AI assistant developed by Anthropic, and conducted what amounted to a wide-ranging policy interview. He pressed Claude on health care, income inequality, corporate greed, and the future of democracy. 

The exchange, posted on YouTube, was remarkable not for what the AI said but for what it revealed: Generative artificial intelligence can engage substantively with complex policy questions more coherently than most witnesses who appear before congressional committees. That should be a wake-up call on Capitol Hill.

Anyone who has watched a congressional hearing in the past two decades, and especially in recent months, knows the ritual. Members of Congress use their allotted five minutes not to ask probing questions but to deliver mini speeches aimed at cable news clips and social media virality. The supposed witnesses, meanwhile, have been coached by armies of lawyers to say as little as possible, deflect with bureaucratic jargon, or simply run out the clock with filibuster-length non-answers. The result is political theater that generates heat but rarely light. The public learns nothing. Accountability is nowhere to be found.

AI will not fix the grandstanding. That is a human choice, and members of Congress have shown no appetite for surrendering their camera time. But AI could fundamentally transform the other side of the equation by improving the quality of the questions being asked and the difficulty of evading them.

Consider what would happen if senators and representatives used a tool like Claude to prepare for hearings. An AI assistant could analyze thousands of pages of depositions, regulatory filings, financial disclosures, and prior testimony in hours.

It could identify contradictions between what a witness said last year and what they are expected to say tomorrow. It could draft sequences of questions designed not as isolated gotcha moments but as logical chains where each answer narrows the witness’s room to maneuver. A well-constructed AI-assisted line of questioning would function less like a scattershot press conference and more like a skilled cross-examination.

Sanders’s conversation with Claude suggests another possibility, allowing him to demonstrate its application in practice. He should use it to help draft questions for all the Senate committees he serves on, and to anticipate likely answers to those questions. If you know how a witness is likely to dodge, you can design the follow-up before the hearing even begins. AI does not get flustered. It does not forget the thread. It can game out five moves ahead while Sanders is still reacting to the first evasion.

This is not hypothetical futurism. The technology exists today. What is missing is the institutional imagination to deploy it. Congressional staff members are overworked, underpaid, and expected to become overnight experts on everything from semiconductor supply chains to social media algorithms to the pricing structures of insulin. They prepare briefing books that members often barely skim before walking into a hearing room. AI would not replace their judgment, but it would radically augment their capacity to prepare members like Sanders, who actually want to hold witnesses accountable rather than simply perform for the cameras.

There are legitimate concerns, of course. AI-generated questions could be manipulated to serve partisan agendas rather than genuine oversight. Members could become lazy, outsourcing their thinking entirely. And there is the uncomfortable question of what it means when an algorithm is better at democratic accountability than the elected officials themselves.

But these risks pale against the status quo, which is a system where hearings have become so performative that most Americans tune them out entirely. When the chair of a major committee cannot get a straight answer from a tech CEO about data privacy or a pharmaceutical executive about drug pricing, the institution itself loses credibility. 

AI will not fix everything. But it could make evasion significantly harder and preparation dramatically deeper. That alone would be worth the experiment.

Sanders stumbled onto something important in his conversation with Claude. He demonstrated that AI can be a serious interlocutor on policy substance. The next step is obvious: bring that capability into congressional hearing rooms. Not as a witness, and certainly not as a replacement for democratic deliberation, but as the best-prepared legislator in the building, who never forgets a prior contradiction or runs out of meaningful follow-up questions.

Stuart N. Brotman is Digital Media Laureate and Distinguished Senior Fellow at The Media Institute. A former Visiting Professor of Entertainment and Media Law at Harvard Law School, he is the author of “Free Expression Under Fire: Defending Free Speech and Free Press Across the Political Spectrum.”

data.title: Blanche boasts of having 'cleaned house' at DOJ, FBI following Trump prosecutions
data.description:
Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche boasted Thursday about the number of firings at the Justice Department (DOJ) and FBI, saying the administration had “cleaned house” by ousting a number of agents that worked on cases involving President Trump.

Blanche, a former defense attorney for Trump, said DOJ had seen the departure of 200 employees between the firings and those who voluntarily left the department.

“There is not a single man or woman at the Department of Justice who had anything to do with those prosecutions,” Blanche said during an interview at the Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC) in Grapevine, Texas.

He went on to say that FBI Director Kash Patel had “cleaned house” by removing a number of agents that also worked on Trump’s prosecutions.

“Director Patel has cleaned house there too,” he added. “There isn’t a single man or woman with a gun — federal agent — still in that organization that had anything to do with the prosecution of President Trump.”

The FBI has hired about 45 agents since Trump returned to the Oval Office last year, which sparked some pushback.

Two unnamed agents sued the bureau last week, arguing they were fired despite exemplary performance records because they had been assigned to work on cases involving the president, including the investigation of the Jan. 6, 2021, attack on the Capitol and the Mar-a-Lago classified documents probe.

Three former high-ranking FBI agents sued Patel last September alleging a “campaign of retribution.” 

That suit included former acting FBI Director Brian Driscoll, who resisted calls from DOJ to turn over a list of agents that worked on Jan. 6 cases.

data.title: Hillary Clinton to keynote New Hampshire event
data.description:
Former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton (D) is set to speak at a major Democratic Party fundraiser in New Hampshire next month, the state party announced on Thursday.

Clinton is set to be the keynote speaker at the New Hampshire Democratic Party’s annual McIntyre-Shaheen 100 Club Dinner on April 25. The announcement was first reported by WMUR.

“Through decades of public service – as First Lady, a U.S. Senator, and Secretary of State – Secretary Clinton has fought tirelessly for women’s rights and been a champion for economic security around the world,” NHDP Chairman Ray Buckley said in a statement to the Manchester, New Hampshire, TV station.

“Her work to expand voting rights, strengthen child and family leave policies, and combat global health crises has made a lasting impact both here and abroad.”

The first 100-Club Dinner was held in 1959 to promote John F. Kennedy’s nomination for president, who was a Massachusetts senator at the time. Since then, it has hosted many current and aspiring officeholders seeking to introduce themselves to voters in the early primary state.

The 2020 dinner featured 10 Democratic presidential candidates, including former President Joe Biden, former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg and Sens. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) and Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), among others.

Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) and Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker (D), both of whom have been speculated as possible 2028 contenders, headlined the dinner in 2023 and 2025, respectively. Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) delivered the keynote address in 2024.

The announcement of Clinton’s appearance at this year’s dinner comes weeks after she and her husband, former President Bill Clinton, were grilled by lawmakers about their past associations with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and his associate Ghislaine Maxwell in a pair of closed-door depositions.

It will be her first public visit to New Hampshire since 2019, when she spoke at Dartmouth College, according to WMUR.

data.title: Top Senate Dems blast proposed ACA rule promoting 'junk coverage' that would push estimated 2 million off enrollment
data.description:
A trio of top Senate Democrats led their colleagues in condemning a proposed rule by the Trump administration that would lift restrictions on the number of non-standardized plans that insurers in the Affordable Care Act (ACA) Marketplace can offer and promotes catastrophic health plans, which are high-deductible plans meant for worst-case scenarios.

In February, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services submitted a proposed rule for the benefit and payment parameters for ACA plans in 2027.

In a letter first shown to The Hill, Democratic Sens. Tammy Baldwin (Wis.), Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) and Ron Wyden (Oreg.) said the rule would cause ACA plans to “increase deductibles, cover fewer services, and kick providers out of network.”

The proposal calls for ending requirements that ACA health plan issuers offer standardized plan options, which require them to offer specific cost sharing structures, while lifting restrictions on how many non-standardized plans Marketplace issuers can offer.

In its proposal, CMS said this rule change would “reduce issuer and HHS burden and regulatory complexity and enhance flexibility for issuers to innovate in plan design.”

States would also be permitted to replace their State-based Exchanged with what the administration referred to as “State Exchange Enhanced Direct Enrollment.” This would allow states to instead use a “private sector-based approach” that relies solely on web brokers to operate consumer-facing websites.

The rule additionally seeks to allow for catastrophic health plans to have terms of multiple years, up to 10. These are plans with low premiums but very high deductibles, generally meant to ensure coverage during unexpected medical emergencies. Catastrophic health plans are normally on annual terms.

In its proposal, CMS stated, “Based on the analysis presented thus far in this section, we expect average enrollment for 2027 to decrease between 1.2 and 2 million enrollees compared to baseline estimates.”

Writing to CMS Administrator Mehmet Oz, the Democratic senators demanded that he withdraw the rule.

“President Trump told the American people he would protect them from the ‘big, fat, rich insurance companies, who have made trillions, and ripped off America long enough,’ they wrote. “Rather than keep this promise, this administration has proposed a rule that CMS’ own experts predict will kick 2 million Americans off the health care they have and accelerate the health care affordability crisis.”

They argued giving states the option to switch to “for-profit web brokers” would promote “junk coverage” and make it harder for consumers to compare plans.

The rule also proposes that health insurers be barred from including adult dental services as part of essential health benefits, services that must be covered under the ACA. The administration said this would follow ACA statutes that require essential health benefits encompass the scope of typical employee-sponsored plans.

The senators said instead of prohibiting essential health services, efforts should be made to ensure more people can access coverage when 72 million adults don’t have dental insurance.

“We should be focused on lowering the cost of care and ensuring that insurance companies cannot raise costs, reduce coverage, and take advantage of working families. We hope that you will join us in this effort by proposing a rule that will help, not harm, Americans and ensure access to affordable and comprehensive health insurance,” wrote the senators.

Other lawmakers who signed the letter include Democratic Sens. Ed Markey (Mass.), Dick Durbin (Ill.), Jeff Merkley (Oreg.), Ben Ray Luján (N.M.), John Hickenlooper (Colo.), Angela Alsobrooks (Md.), Alex Padilla (Calif.), Michael Bennet (Colo.), Chris Van Hollen (Md.), Patty Murray (Wash.), Elizabeth Warren (Mass.), Richard Blumenthal (Conn.), Lisa Blunt Rochester (Del.) and Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.)

data.title: Trump housing chief seeks new criminal probe into Letitia James
data.description:
Bill Pulte, director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, is seeking a new criminal probe into New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) after failing to convict her on mortgage fraud charges last year.

Two criminal referrals submitted by Pulte and reviewed by NewsNation allege that James made false representations about who would occupy her homes in Illinois and Florida, respectively. 

Pulte alleged that James may have falsified information on her homeowners insurance application to Universal Property Insurance by stating that her property would be unoccupied five months out of the year. Pulte cited documents posted to X by attorney Mike Davis, who has gained traction as a pro-Trump conservative media personality.

Trump’s housing chief also alleged that James told Allstate her property in Illinois would be occupied by a single adult, with no children. 

Instead, the home was occupied by four people — three children and her niece, according to documents posted by Davis.

Pulte submitted criminal referrals regarding James’s home insurance filings to Jason Reding Quinones, Trump’s nominee for U.S. Attorney of the Southern District of Florida and Andrew Boutros, nominee for U.S. Attorney of the Northern District of Illinois.

Last October, James was indicted by a federal grand jury on two charges stemming from mortgage fraud allegations submitted by Pulte. However, a judge later dismissed the charges after determining the appointment of Lindsey Halligan as acting U.S. attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia was unlawful. 

In December, two other grand juries failed to indict James on the mortgage fraud allegations. 

James’s office did not immediately respond to The Hill’s request for comment regarding the new criminal referrals.
